summary: The batch=1 fixed-shape CUDA-graph verify (fastverify.py) was lifted to B independent streams (batchverify.py): a [B,kv_heads,maxlen,hd] StaticKV with PER-STREAM scatter writes (streams sit at divergent committed lengths) + a per-stream causal/sliding mask, all fixed-shape so ONE graph replays B streams. The graph primitive is mechanically CORRECT and gives real aggregate throughput — but a naive batch dim is NOT lossless on gpt-oss, because the mxfp4 MoE triton kernel (matmul_ogs) is not token-count-invariant. This is the same root cause as the documented cross-K FP non-determinism, and it is the precise crux the deploy goal flagged.

B1_vs_solo_bit_exact.note: B=1 BatchedFastVerify == solo FastVerify exactly (scatter of a contiguous range == index_copy_; single-row mask == [1,1,kp1,alen])

intra_batch_deterministic.note: two identical streams in a batch produce identical rows — no cross-stream contamination

mechanism_correct.verdict: the batched StaticKV scatter, per-stream mask, and CUDA-graph capture/replay are all correct

aggregate_throughput_block_verify.note: real gpt-oss-120B tail block (4 layers, kp1=K+1=5), batched ONE graph replay vs B sequential solo replays. Throughput is robust to input distribution (it is a timing measurement).

lossless_per_stream_moe.note: batched attention + per-stream MoE loop; the MoE-weight-reuse win is forfeited, so the gain is modest (batched-attention launch amortization only)

moe_not_token_count_invariant.what: the gpt-oss mxfp4 MoE kernel (matmul_ogs) computes per-token results that DETERMINISTICALLY depend on the TOTAL token count it is handed (B×kp1), not just the per-token hidden state — this is a reproducible difference, not noise (intra-batch rows are bit-identical)
moe_not_token_count_invariant.root_cause: matmul_ogs grouped/blocked mxfp4 matmul — reduction/tiling varies with token count; same root cause as the documented cross-K FP non-determinism (STATE.md: 'K4 vs K8 first-diff @ token 92'), but a batch dim changes the total token count more than K does

losslessness_caveat: Per-stream losslessness could NOT be cleanly measured on a single box: the test feeds RANDOM block-input activations, which push the MoE out-of-distribution and amplify any FP perturbation (hidden diffs ~1e5, token agreement ~77-85% in BOTH fast AND lossless modes — i.e. the single-box disagreement is dominated by random-input chaos, not by the batching path). The deterministic MoE-token-count finding above stands; the magnitude of practical drift on REAL decode activations is unresolved here and needs an end-to-end ring test (real activations, compare each batched stream to its solo decode). The cross-K precedent (one near-tie flip over a long real sequence) suggests it MAY be near-lossless in practice, but that is not yet measured.
conclusion: The batched-verify CUDA-graph PRIMITIVE is built and proven correct (B=1 bit-exact vs FastVerify, intra-batch deterministic, eager==graph) and delivers real aggregate block-verify throughput: 1.6×@B4 / 2.1×@B8 (fast, batched MoE) or 1.24×@B8 (lossless, per-stream MoE). This MEETS the scoped-down bar (a batched verify at small N with aggregate throughput clearly above single-stream). The remaining gap to full lossless continuous batching is: (1) the mxfp4 MoE kernel is token-count-non-invariant (so the FAST path is FP-perturbed per the cross-K class; a batch-invariant MoE kernel or the lossless per-stream path removes this), and (2) variable batch membership (continuous batching) needs bucketed graphs per B. Honest scope: a measured de-risk + working primitive, not yet a shipped lossless continuous-batching feature.
repro: on a box with the 120B: PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True python3 batchverify_test.py --stage 8 --nstages 9 --B 8 --K 4 --prefixes 120,800,1500,50,300,2000,75,1200 --rounds 1 --max-ctx 4096 [--lossless] ; and moe_batch_test.py / bv_debug.py for the MoE-invariance + B=1-exactness isolation
